summary: Stancil uses the deceptively simple question of 'how do you make a chart with AI' to illustrate a profound tension in the software industry: AI's increasing capability threatens to collapse the entire software supply chain by working at lower and lower levels of abstraction. He explores whether AI will replace visualization tools, databases, and infrastructure — or whether the hard-won solutions embedded in existing software remain indispensable.
key_points: AI's growing capabilities raise the question of how far down the software supply chain it can replace existing tools and companies
MotherDuck's Dives and similar products suggest AI can bypass dedicated visualization software by generating JavaScript directly
Existing software solves hard edge-case problems (overlapping labels, misplaced legends) that seem trivial until you encounter them
The real future of analytics might not be better charts but AI-powered 'vibes' — interpreting unstructured data qualitatively
SpaceX's proposed acquisition of Cursor for $60B represents the absurd gravity of AI coding tools as an attention singularity
money_quotes: Everything that is done on a computer passes through an entire supply chain of software, a supply chain that supports thousands of businesses and millions of jobs.
Software isn't just a convenient shorthand for doing something; it solves the very hard problems that you don't realize exist until you have them.

If they can work around all of the headaches that Tableau has spent twenty years solving, could they not also work around the headaches that make it hard to host those visualizations? Could they not build a better database?
I don't know AI coding agents are a technological singularity, but they are definitely the attention singularity.
There are no overlapping axes, or misplaced legends, or Rubik's cubes full of frustrations. The vibes are much less of a headache than the analytics.
key_insight: AI's ability to work at increasingly lower levels of software abstraction threatens to collapse the entire supply chain that the tech industry is built on, but the line between 'useful shortcut' and 'absurd overreach' remains impossible to draw.
